key_points: Electric current produces both heating and magnetic effects when it flows through a conductor
The heating effect occurs because electrical energy converts into heat due to resistance in the conductor
The magnetic effect of electric current creates a magnetic field around a current-carrying wire
An electromagnet is created when electric current flows through a coil wrapped around a soft iron core
Electromagnets are temporary magnets and lose magnetism when current stops
Safety devices like fuses protect circuits from damage caused by excessive current
examples: Electric heaters and irons work using the heating effect of electric current
A fuse wire melts when too much current flows through the circuit
A compass needle deflects when placed near a current-carrying wire
Electric cranes use electromagnets to lift heavy iron objects
Electric bells use electromagnets to produce sound
formulas: Pressure-like relation in circuits: Heat produced ∝ current × resistance (conceptual relation)
Magnetic field forms around a current-carrying conductor
Strength of electromagnet increases with number of coil turns and current
exam_tips: Explain clearly the difference between heating effect and magnetic effect of current
Use examples like electric bell and electric crane to explain electromagnets
Remember why fuse wires have low melting points
Understand how increasing current or coil turns affects electromagnet strength
